narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 42% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. Your profile shows you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in your first year of school and the third decile by your third year—both notably lower than the typical student's mid-range positions around the fifth or sixth decile. Your LSAT score of 35 was a bit below the average of about 37, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.2 lined up closely with the usual 3.21. Coming from an upper-middle-income family put you roughly in line with peers, and as a white male planning full-time studies, you match the most common demographic patterns in our dataset, where these traits are held by the large majority of students. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your third-year decile rank, which had the strongest pull toward predicting bar exam struggles for you specifically. Right behind that was your first-year decile, followed by your LSAT score, family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—in that order of influence. Each of these factors subtly nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success, with your lower early academic rankings standing out as the biggest contributors compared to typical students who tend to perform better consistently. While no single element was overwhelmingly decisive, the combination painted a picture of potential challenges in sustaining the high performance needed for the bar. The good news is that your story isn't set in stone—many students with similar starting points turn things around. To boost your future odds, focus on building stronger study habits and academic consistency, perhaps by seeking tutoring or bar prep courses tailored to overcome early-grade weaknesses like those reflected in your deciles. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also shift the balance, as it's one area where improvement is realistic and impactful. With targeted effort, you can outperform this prediction and position yourself for success next time. Keep pushing forward—you've got the determination to make this right.
